upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a very shallow, moist boundary layer with a near-saturated surface (temp/dewpoint spread of only 1.9°C at 1014mb), suggesting coastal marine influence and fog/mist at the surface. The lapse rate surface to 850mb is only -4.39°C/km — well below dry adiabatic (9.8) and even below moist adiabatic (~6.5), indicating a strong stable layer and essentially no thermal potential today. Winds at 850mb are light (3 kts from 250°) veering to 260°/16 kts at 700mb, with no significant upper-level shear hazard, but the stable profile will cap boundary layer height near 350-400m (roughly 1150-1300 ft) through midday.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area SF region. KHAF reporting mist (BR) with 5-6SM visibility and calm winds through 1355Z — marine layer holding at the coast. KRHV calm/SKC at 10°C by 1355Z — inland clearing underway. KSFO light W at 3 kts, CLR. KAPC light NE 4 kts, CLR. KWVI light E at 3 kts, CLR. Buoy 46012 (Half Moon Bay) reporting 13.6 kts from 310° with 2.8m/9s swell and SST 12.8°C — cold SST confirms a robust marine layer slow to erode at coastal sites.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft — Summit Launch)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas/Fremont hills — NNW-facing ridge, thermal and ridge hybrid site
sites.howItWorks: The summit launch at 1750 ft works best with W to NW sea breeze for ridge soaring, and thermal cycles driven by inland heating. Ideal wind is 8-15 kts from 270-320°; winds from N/NE in the morning are normal valley drainage and typically cycle to W/NW by midday.
sites.todaySetup: The key problem at the summit today is a very strong NNE gradient wind at altitude during the morning hours: HRRR shows 80m winds at 21-24 kts from NNE at 8-10 AM, which is well above the comfortable limit and cross/back to the launch. This is driven by the overnight drainage and residual gradient, not sea breeze, and makes early launch dangerous. The stable sounding (lapse rate only -4.4°C/km) kills any meaningful thermal development — BL tops out near 350-400m AGL, well below the 1750 ft launch elevation. By midday clouds build to 90-94% coverage, further suppressing thermals. Surface winds do lighten and back toward NW through the afternoon (6-7 kts by 1-5 PM) as the sea breeze asserts itself, but the weak thermal environment and persistent cloud cover make conditions marginal at best. The 80m winds aloft remain stronger than surface readings (8-10 kts at 80m by afternoon), suggesting some shear in the column. Not a day to fly the summit for most pilots.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Surface winds 11-15 kts from NNE (30-35°), 80m winds a dangerous 20-24 kts from NNE — well above limits and back to launch. Do not launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds ease and back to N/NNW then NW (7-13 kts surface), but cloud cover surges to 90-94%, killing thermals. Conditions technically lighter but sky is socked in and uninspiring.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds settle to 6-7 kts from WNW-NW (310-330°) — a better direction — and clouds partially break by 3 PM. However, BL height remains shallow (~380m) and thermals are weak to nonexistent given the stable sounding.

verdict.bestWindow: 2:30 PM – 5 PM (if clouds break as forecast)
verdict.hazards: Strong NNE winds aloft 8-10 AM (20+ kts at 80m), 90%+ cloud cover midday killing thermals, shallow BL capping thermal height, wind shear between surface and 80m layer afternoon

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft — LZ / Lower Launch)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas foothills — lower NW-facing training slope
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft lower launch is a training/sledder hill primarily used for short soaring in the valley breeze and as an LZ reference. It benefits from the same W/NW sea breeze cycle as the summit but at much lower altitude. Ideal for P2 practice in 5-10 kt W/NW flow.
sites.todaySetup: This site tells a similar story to the summit but with more favorable numbers: morning winds are NNE at 8-12 kts at 10m (less severe than aloft), easing and cycling to NW by afternoon. The key limiting factor is the stable atmosphere — zero thermal development given a -4.4°C/km lapse rate — meaning any flight here is a sledder or short ridge soar, not a thermal flight. Cloudcover hits 76-93% from 11 AM to 2 PM, keeping heating suppressed. Afternoon does improve with the wind cycling to 6-8 kts NW/WNW and clouds breaking, offering a reasonable sledder window. The 80m winds at 9-10 kts during afternoon suggest some upper flow that could be bumpy for low-altitude training. Best used as a sledder or P2 practice hill in the afternoon window.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Surface winds 7-12 kts from NNE/N — light to moderate but wrong direction for this NW-facing site, making the hill unfavorable. Avoid.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds lighten to 4-6 kts, cycling toward NW by 1 PM. Heavy cloud cover (91-93%) keeps thermals suppressed — sledder only conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: winds 6-8 kts from WNW-NW (310-330°), clouds clearing. Suitable for short ridge/sledder flights. 80m winds 9 kts suggest moderate upper flow — stay alert to cycles.

verdict.hazards: Wrong direction morning winds, heavy midday cloud suppressing any thermals, shallow BL, some upper shear in afternoon

sites.name: Ed Levin (300 ft — Bunny Hill)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas valley floor training hill — W-facing slope
sites.howItWorks: The 300 ft bunny hill is a ground-handling and beginner flight area, relying on gentle W/NW valley breeze. Ideal in 5-10 kts W/WNW with stable, smooth conditions — purely a training site.
sites.todaySetup: The 300 ft site sees the cleanest afternoon window of the three Ed Levin launches today. Morning winds at 10m are very light (2-5 kts from WSW/W) — below usable lift threshold early on. By midday the wind cycles to W at 5-8 kts, building toward 8 kts from WNW-NW (295-315°) in the afternoon — a solid training breeze. The stable atmosphere is actually a feature here, not a bug: smooth, predictable air is ideal for beginners. Cloud cover clears significantly by 3 PM, and BL height of 380-415m provides adequate ceiling for this low altitude site. The 80m winds track at 8-10 kts in the afternoon, suggesting a bit more punch at altitude, but at 300 ft this site is largely sheltered from upper-column shear. Best training conditions of the day across Ed Levin.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Very light winds 2-5 kts from WSW (260°) — too light and slightly wrong direction. Ground handling practice only.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds build to 5-8 kts W/WNW, 90% cloud cover midday. Usable for training flights but sky is overcast.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: 8 kts WNW-NW (295-315°), clouds breaking by 3 PM. Smooth stable air ideal for P2 training and sledders.

verdict.hazards: Light/variable morning winds, midday overcast. Afternoon conditions stable and benign. Minimal hazards for the appropriate skill level.

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W/NW-facing ridge lift site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge soaring site energized by the Pacific sea breeze and swell-driven onshore flow. It needs 10-20 kts from W to NNW to produce reliable lift along the bluff. Too light and there is no lift; too strong (20+ kts) and it becomes dangerous. Marine layer timing and burn-off are critical.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock is marginal today due to a combination of light morning winds and delayed sea breeze onset. The Half Moon Bay METAR shows mist and calm winds at 1355Z, confirming the marine layer is sitting on the coast and suppressing the westerly flow that drives this site. NDBC buoy 46012 reports SST of 12.8°C with 2.8m/9s swell — cold water means a deeper, slower-burning marine layer. Surface winds at the site are only 3-6 kts from WSW/W through 11 AM, well below the 8-10 kt threshold for reliable ridge lift. Winds do build through the afternoon to a more workable 8-9 kts from W/WNW (270-280°) by 2-5 PM as the sea breeze fills in, but GFS/HRRR both show 80m winds running 9-11 kts — reasonable but not epic ridge conditions. The 2% precip flag at 13:00-19:00 UTC and patchy cloud (66% at 1 PM) suggest a messy, unsettled flow rather than clean laminar ridge lift. An acceptable afternoon window exists for experienced P3 pilots, but it is not a classic Mussel Rock soaring day.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light and variable 2-4 kts from SW/W — marine layer holding, no ridge lift. Not flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Sea breeze beginning to fill in, 5-8 kts from W by noon to 1 PM. Borderline lift threshold — scratchy conditions at best.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: 8-9 kts W/WNW (270-280°), 80m winds 9-11 kts. Ridge lift developing but patchy clouds and minor precip flag suggest turbulent, unsteady flow. P3 pilots only.

verdict.hazards: Marine layer suppressing morning flow, cold SST slowing burn-off, patchy clouds and 2% precip flag in afternoon suggesting turbulent sea breeze, light morning winds below lift threshold

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — NW-facing ridge and thermal launch, inland bay hills
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak is a NW/W-facing launch at ~2500 ft that works on both thermal and ridge mechanisms. Ideal in W to NW flow 8-18 kts with active thermals cycling up the face. Morning drainage winds from NE/N are normal. It shares the same inland valley setting as Ed Levin and responds similarly to the sea breeze cycle.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak faces the same critical problem as Ed Levin Summit: extremely strong NNE winds aloft in the morning. HRRR shows 80m winds at 21-24 kts from NNE at 8-10 AM — dangerous and back to launch. The stable sounding with a -4.4°C/km lapse rate means thermal ceilings are capped near 350-380m AGL, and the 92-94% cloud cover from 11 AM to 2 PM completely shuts down solar heating. Wind direction improves through the afternoon, cycling to NNW then NW (327-313°) by 3-5 PM with surface speeds of 6-7 kts — this is the only viable window. The GFS 120m winds (representing ridge-top and above) show 8-10 kts from WNW in the afternoon, which is better aligned for ridge lift, but the shallow BL and weak thermals mean altitude gain will be limited. The afternoon window (3-5 PM) is usable for experienced pilots who want a short soar, but not a high-performance XC day.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 80m winds 20-24 kts from NNE (northeast) — dangerous, back to launch, well above limits. Absolutely do not launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Surface winds ease to 6-9 kts cycling toward NNW, but 90-94% cloud cover kills thermal development. Winds are improving in direction but sky is socked in.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: 6-7 kts from NW-WNW (310-327°) with cloud clearing after 3 PM. Weak thermals possible, 80m winds 7-8 kts. Short soaring flight realistic for experienced pilots.

verdict.hazards: Dangerous 20+ kt NNE winds aloft 8-10 AM, heavy cloud cover 11 AM–2 PM suppressing thermals, shallow BL (~380m) capping altitude gain, wind shear between surface and 80m layer in morning

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday March 22 shows 100% cloud cover at 8 AM with a near-zero BL height (60m), suggesting overnight marine intrusion or fog. By midday clouds persist at 88-91% and winds remain light (3-8 kts) from variable directions. Afternoon sees partial clearing and W/WNW flow building to 8 kts — marginally better than today but still suppressed thermals. Precip risk at 1-2% through the day. MARGINAL at best.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Similar story to the summit — overcast morning with 100% clouds and shallow BL. Afternoon offers the best window with W/NW flow 6-8 kts and partial clearing. Light precip risk (1-2%). Marginal P2 training conditions in the afternoon.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Overcast morning, gradual improvement through afternoon with W/NW 7-8 kts by 2-4 PM. Light precip risk. Afternoon training window possible but less reliable than today's afternoon window.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday looks worse than today for Mussel Rock — 100% cloud cover persists through the entire day per HRRR, with W/NW winds building to 9-10 kts by afternoon. The persistent overcast and 1-2% precip suggest a marine push/overrunning moisture. Not a recommended fly day.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday mirrors Ed Levin — overcast start, light variable winds through midday, afternoon improvement toward W/NW 5-8 kts. Similar marginal/no-fly morning and marginal afternoon window for experienced pilots only.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday March 23 looks significantly better: clear skies (0-6% cloud), calm morning transitioning to W/WNW 5-8 kts by noon and building to 8+ kts by afternoon. BL height reaches 500-675m — much healthier for thermals. Light, buoyant air. FLY day potential if the sea breeze fills in cleanly.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday clears nicely with 0-6% cloud cover and BL heights of 500-675m. W/WNW flow 5-8 kts afternoon. Good P2 training and moderate thermal soaring day.

day2.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday looks like the cleanest day of the 4-day period for the bunny hill. Clear skies, light morning winds building to WNW 8 kts afternoon, stable but deeper BL. Excellent P2 training day.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Monday shows a promising window: clear morning, W/NW flow building from 4-6 kts midday to 8-9 kts by 2-4 PM. 100% cloud after 4 PM is a concern. Best flyable window likely 12-4 PM if sea breeze arrives on time.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Monday is the best day of the outlook for Mission Peak — clear skies, light calm morning, building W/NW flow to 5 kts surface (but 80m winds 7-11 kts by afternoon), BL heights 520-670m. Weak but real thermals possible. Recommend monitoring.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Tuesday March 24 looks similar to Monday: calm morning, building W/NW sea breeze 5-9 kts afternoon, clear skies, BL height 620-865m — the deepest of the 4-day period. Good thermal flying potential. Best XC day of the outlook.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Tuesday continues the improving trend with BL heights to 865m and clear conditions. W/NW flow 5-9 kts. Strong P2 and P3 flying day.

day3.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day3.analysis: Tuesday looks excellent for the bunny hill: clear, light morning, 8-9 kts WNW afternoon, deepest BL of the week. Ideal P2 training conditions.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Tuesday shows winds building to 11-14 kts from WNW/W by midday and afternoon — this is approaching the upper end of comfortable ridge soaring. 61-76% cloud cover midday may limit thermal component. Could be epic ridge soaring or too strong depending on exact timing. P3+ pilots only.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Tuesday shows the best thermal potential of the week: BL heights 595-725m, clear skies, W flow 4-5 kts surface but 80m winds building to 8-11 kts by afternoon. The upper-column winds are stronger than surface, suggesting valley wind shear — watch for rotor risk on approach. P3 pilots, strong day.

watchlist: Marine layer at KHAF persisting through late morning (mist, 5-6SM vis, calm winds at 1355Z) — coastal sites may not see usable sea breeze until 1-2 PM at earliest
Cold SST (12.8°C at buoy 46012) and 2.8m/9s swell confirm robust marine layer — Mussel Rock burn-off timing uncertain, could stay socked in
Strong NNE gradient winds aloft (20-24 kts at 80m) at Ed Levin and Mission Peak through 10 AM — extreme hazard for early launchers
Stable sounding (-4.4°C/km lapse rate) caps thermals well below summit launch heights — no meaningful thermal flying today
2% precip flags at Mussel Rock from 1-7 PM UTC suggest unsettled coastal flow — watch for squalls or rotor turbulence
Monday-Tuesday improving trend is the real flying opportunity this weekend — consider rest day Saturday and plan for Monday
tldr: Today is mostly a no-go for serious flying — strong NNE winds aloft hammer Ed Levin and Mission Peak all morning, the stable sounding kills thermals, and the marine layer is keeping Mussel Rock asleep. If you must fly, the Ed Levin 300 ft bunny hill offers a clean P2 training window from 2-5 PM with 7-8 kts WNW. Save your energy for Monday and Tuesday — those look like the actual fly days this week.
